_meta.purpose: Maintainer-authored stat reference. Drives the Stat School tab + the dotted-term deep-link targets across the dashboard.

ops.name: On-base Plus Slugging
ops.definition_md: Two skills in one number: how often a hitter reaches base (OBP) plus how much power they hit for (SLG). The fastest one-glance read on a bat.
ops.frame_line_md: On this dashboard OPS always carries its MLB rank. Guerrero Jr.'s .963 reads as 3rd of 30 qualified 1B — elite, not just "good."

obp.name: On-base Percentage
obp.definition_md: How often a hitter reaches base via hit, walk, or hit-by-pitch. The most reliable single-number read on plate skill.

avg.name: Batting Average
avg.definition_md: Hits per at-bat. The oldest stat — easy to read, but blind to walks, power, and luck. We show it for orientation; OPS or xwOBA tells you more.

slg.name: Slugging Percentage
slg.definition_md: Total bases per at-bat. Captures power: a single is worth 1, a homer is worth 4. Reads as the power half of OPS.

xwoba.name: Expected Weighted On-Base Average
xwoba.definition_md: What a hitter deserved based on how hard and at what angle they hit the ball — stripping out luck, defense, and ballpark. Compare it to actual results to see who's due.
xwoba.frame_line_md: Shown with its MLB percentile — the one Statcast frame this dashboard uses, because xwOBA is published with it.
xwoba.formula_md: xwOBA = f( exit velocity, launch angle ) → expected value of each batted ball, weighted like wOBA
xwoba.why_md: Why it beats batting average: AVG treats a 105-mph lineout and a bloop single as equal-but-opposite. xwOBA asks what usually happens on a ball hit that hard at that angle — so a hitter with bad luck and a great xwOBA is a buy-low, not a slump.

barrel_pct.name: Barrel Rate
barrel_pct.definition_md: Percentage of batted balls hit at the optimal combination of exit velocity and launch angle to produce extra-base hits. The cleanest power signal Statcast publishes.

era.name: Earned Run Average
era.definition_md: Average runs earned against a pitcher per nine innings. Excludes runs from defensive errors. The first stat anyone learns about pitching — and the one most affected by the defense behind them.
era.frame_line_md: ERA always carries its MLB rank here. A 3.21 starter ERA reads 7th of 30 — that's a top-of-rotation tier.

fip.name: Fielding Independent Pitching
fip.definition_md: An ERA-style number built only from what a pitcher controls alone — strikeouts, walks, and home runs — ignoring the defense behind them.
fip.why_md: Why it's here: if a pitcher's ERA and FIP disagree, the gap is usually defense or luck — and it tends to close. A low FIP behind a high ERA is a bounce-back signal.

whip.name: Walks + Hits per Inning Pitched
whip.definition_md: How many baserunners the pitcher allows per inning. Simple, durable, and a clean read on traffic.

run_differential.name: Run Differential
run_differential.definition_md: Runs scored minus runs allowed across the season. The single best one-number read on team quality — better than record on short samples.
run_differential.frame_line_md: Shown with its MLB rank on the Overview KPI card. Toronto's +28 reads 8th of 30 — quietly contender-level, even when the record looks middling.

pythag.name: Pythagorean Expectation
pythag.definition_md: The record a team's run differential says it should have. Teams that beat or trail it usually drift back toward it — so it hints where the season is really heading.
pythag.frame_line_md: On the Overview, pace and Pythag sit side by side on purpose: the gap is the regression to watch, shown honestly rather than rounded away.
pythag.formula_md: Win% ≈ RS^2 / (RS^2 + RA^2)  →  ×162 for a projected wins total

mlb_rank.name: MLB Rank — the context standard
mlb_rank.definition_md: Where the team or player sits among all 30 clubs (or all qualified players). 1st is best, 30th is worst. That frame turns a bare number into "is this good?" without you having to memorise league averages. Every stat on this dashboard carries its rank wherever the MLB Stats API exposes one.

honesty.title: Beyond this dashboard
honesty.body_md: You'll hear about WAR and wRC+ — good stats, but this dashboard doesn't show them: there's no free, public feed that computes WAR, and inventing our own would be guessing. We'd rather show you nothing than show you a number we can't stand behind. For those, FanGraphs and Baseball Savant are the places to grow into next.
honesty.byline: The maintainer · why we draw the line here

pitches.name: Four-seam Fastball
pitches.description_md: Straightest, fastest pitch. Looks like it "rises" — it just drops less than the eye expects.

pitches.name: Two-seam Fastball / Sinker
pitches.description_md: Drops and runs toward the pitcher's arm side. Made to get ground balls.

pitches.name: Cutter
pitches.description_md: A fastball with a late, small glove-side cut. Jams hitters and breaks bats.

pitches.name: Slider
pitches.description_md: Sweeps glove-side and down. The go-to swing-and-miss pitch.

pitches.name: Curveball
pitches.description_md: Big top-to-bottom drop. Slowest pitch — meant to freeze or fool timing.

pitches.name: Changeup
pitches.description_md: Looks like a fastball, arrives slow and fades arm-side. Beats a hitter's timing.
